entries.title: Hugging Face Discloses First End-to-End AI-Agent-Driven Production Breach — and Found Its Own Safety Guardrails Impeded Defense
entries.summary: An autonomous AI agent exploited RCE and template-injection vulnerabilities in Hugging Face's dataset processing pipeline to harvest cloud/cluster credentials across multiple internal clusters; defenders found that commercial frontier model APIs blocked their forensic requests containing real exploit payloads, forcing them to pivot to the open-weight GLM 5.2 — a concrete demonstration that offensive agentic AI operates without usage restrictions while defensive AI is constrained by the same safety measures labs have built.

links.title: Hugging Face Security Incident Disclosure (official blog)
The Hacker News: World's Largest AI Model Repository Breached by Autonomous AI Agent
AI Weekly: Hugging Face Discloses AI-Agent-Driven Breach of Internal Clusters

entries.title: EU Issues Binding DMA Orders: Rival AI Assistants Get Equal Android System Access and Google Search Data Starting January 2027
entries.summary: The European Commission adopted two specification decisions on July 16 under the Digital Markets Act, requiring Google to open 11 Android integration points (voice activation, cross-app actions, screen context) to third-party AI assistants like ChatGPT and Claude on the same terms it grants Gemini, and to share anonymized Google Search data with rival AI developers including OpenAI starting January 2027 — the most consequential EU antitrust intervention directly reshaping the AI competitive landscape.

links.title: Unite.AI: EU Compels Google to Share Android and Search Data With Rival AI Assistants
TechTimes: EU Gives Rival AI Assistants System-Level Android Access Google Reserved for Gemini

entries.title: EU AI Act August 2 Deadline: What Actually Applies — Omnibus Defers High-Risk Obligations 17 Months, But Transparency Rules and AI Office Enforcement Powers Take Effect Now
entries.summary: A July 17 analysis clarifies that the EU AI Act Digital Omnibus (formally agreed but not yet published in the Official Journal as of July 17) splits August 2 into two tiers: transparency obligations (chatbot disclosure, synthetic content watermarking, AI Office GPAI penalty powers up to €15M) activate on schedule, while Annex III high-risk system compliance — recruitment tools, credit scoring, law enforcement — is deferred to December 2, 2027; critically, if the Omnibus is not formally published before August 2, the original high-risk deadlines remain legally binding.

links.title: Technology.org: EU AI Act — What Actually Applies on 2 August 2026
EU AI Act Official Implementation Timeline (EC)

entries.title: Nature News: UK AISI Emerges as Decisive Global AI Safety Evaluation Hub — Evaluated Anthropic Mythos Before Public Clarity Existed
entries.summary: A July 16 Nature News feature documents that the UK AI Security Institute has become a critical frontier model evaluation hub, illustrated by its being the body that provided 'real clarity' about Anthropic Mythos's power after its launch; London now hosts Anthropic and OpenAI major offices alongside Apollo Research and GovAI, positioning the UK as a distinct third pole — safety-focused rather than model-focused — in the global AI landscape.

links.title: Nature News: A global capital for AI safety is emerging — and it's not in Silicon Valley

entries.title: arXiv 2607.15992: The Case for Independent Certification for Trustworthy AI — Closing the 'Trust Gap'
entries.summary: A new multi-author arXiv preprint (Papakonstantinou, Canca, Nanji, Weedon, Ball et al.) argues that voluntary commitments and self-attestation are insufficient to close the AI trust gap and makes the case for independent, third-party certification of AI systems as a governance mechanism — directly relevant to ongoing debates about mandatory third-party audits in the GAAIA and EU AI Act.

links.title: arXiv 2607.15992: Closing the AI Trust Gap: The Case for Independent Certification for Trustworthy AI

entries.title: arXiv 2607.16112: Harmonizing AI Safety Thresholds
entries.summary: A new arXiv preprint by Anterola, Ball, Lafuerza, and Grey (cs.AI) addresses the problem of reconciling divergent safety thresholds across AI governance frameworks — a core challenge for international AI governance coordination as jurisdictions proliferate competing threshold definitions for high-risk and frontier AI systems.

entries.title: Illinois Governor Signs AI Safety Measures Act — First US State to Mandate Third-Party Safety Audits for Frontier Developers
entries.summary: Illinois Governor Pritzker has signed SB 315 (the Artificial Intelligence Safety Measures Act), enacting the first US state law requiring annual independent safety plan audits specifically for frontier AI model developers with over $500 million in annual revenue, with an effective date of January 1, 2027, civil penalties up to $3 million per violation, exclusive Attorney General enforcement, and no private right of action — adding a third pillar to the California/New York state governance patchwork.

links.title: AI Governance Institute: China Agent Rules Take Effect July 15 and Illinois Mandates Third-Party Safety Audits
Mintz: AI Washington Report — July 2026 Edition

entries.title: Apple v. OpenAI and io Products: Trade Secret Theft Complaint Alleges Coordinated Scheme to Steal Hardware IP Across 400+ Former Employees
entries.summary: Apple filed a 41-page complaint in N.D. Cal. on July 10 alleging that OpenAI's Chief Hardware Officer Tang Tan systematically directed Apple employees to bring physical hardware parts to interviews, coached departing Apple staff to evade exit security, and circulated confidential Apple documents internally — with io Products (Jony Ive's firm, acquired by OpenAI for $6.5B) also named — making this the most significant AI-company-vs-AI-company IP dispute to date and potentially threatening OpenAI's device roadmap.

links.title: TechCrunch: Apple sues OpenAI over alleged trade secret theft
